notes: Irregular. Also used as auxiliary verb.

notes: Very versatile verb. Can mean stay, become, be located.
Always feminine regardless of child's gender.
Also used as a term of endearment: meu amor.
As adjective, agrees in gender: muito/muita.
Used for comparatives: mais grande = bigger.

exampleTranslation: I have never been to Japan.

notes: Free as in available or liberty, not price.
Contracts: de + o = do, de + a = da.
Contracts: em + o = no, em + a = na.
Comigo = with me, contigo = with you.
Contracts: por + o = pelo, por + a = pela.
Standard Brazilian Portuguese 'you'. Uses 3rd person verb forms.
'A gente' is very common in informal speech.

exampleTranslation: I want to, but I can't.

notes: Used both as greeting and farewell at night.
Used to get attention politely or to pass through.
Até mais = see you later (more casual).

notes: Polite way to order or request.

exampleTranslation: I need to buy new clothes.

exampleTranslation: Rice and beans is the base of Brazilian food.

notes: Comparative of 'bom'. Same form for m/f.
